extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The Physical Twin is a small‑scale thermal incubator consisting of an insulated styrofoam box equipped with a heatbed, a 0.02 m³/s fan for air circulation, three DS18S20 high‑precision 1‑Wire temperature sensors, a Raspberry Pi 4 Model B acting as the controller, and a custom PCB that drives the heatbed via MOSFETs isolated by optocouplers. The incubator is designed for biological experiments that require a stable temperature environment and is fully open‑source in both hardware and software.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin provides real‑time monitoring and visualization of the incubator status by ingesting controller and low‑level driver messages into a time‑series database and rendering them on a dashboard. It offers on‑demand calibration of two thermal models (Model A with two free parameters and Model B with four free parameters) through automated calibration routines. Anomaly detection and heat‑bed state estimation are performed with a Kalman filter that fuses measured sensor data with Model B predictions. What‑if simulations are enabled via co‑simulation of the physical and digital models, allowing optimisation of controller parameters (LL, UL, H, C). Finally, a self‑adaptation loop automatically detects behavioural changes, schedules experiments, recalibrates the model, re‑configures the Kalman filter and controller, and iterates to maintain optimal operation.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: Implementation relies on RabbitMQ as the message broker for synchronisation between the Raspberry Pi controller, low‑level driver, and Digital Twin components. The controller runs on a Raspberry Pi 4 Model B and implements a bang‑bang control strategy with parameters LL, UL, H, and C. The low‑level driver abstracts sensor and actuator communication, periodically reading DS18S20 temperatures and publishing them to RabbitMQ. The heatbed is driven via a custom PCB that uses MOSFETs and optocouplers. A Kalman filter is used for anomaly detection and state estimation. Co‑simulation is employed for what‑if analysis, and a time‑series database stores historical data for real‑time visualization. All source code, hardware designs, and datasets are released under an open‑source license on GitHub (https://github.com/INTO‑CPS‑Association/example-incubator).
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The DT system is orchestrated around an asynchronous message broker (RabbitMQ) that decouples the physical twin (PT) components – a Raspberry Pi‑based controller and a low‑level driver – from the digital twin services. The controller consumes temperature readings published by the low‑level driver, executes a bang‑bang control loop, and publishes control commands back to RabbitMQ. Digital twin services subscribe to the same message stream: a Kalman‑filter module estimates the unmeasured heat‑bed temperature, a co‑simulation engine performs what‑if analyses using the calibrated Model B, and a self‑adaptation loop monitors anomalies, schedules experiments, recalibrates the model, and re‑configures the controller. All services run as independent processes (or containers) on the same host or distributed across the network, sharing a time‑series database for real‑time visualization. This event‑driven, message‑centric architecture enables modular scaling and clear separation of responsibilities between the PT and DT layers.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The DT constellation for the incubator comprises two analytical thermal models: Model A (two free parameters – C_air and G_box) and Model B (four free parameters – C_air, G_box, C_heater, G_heater).  Both models are calibrated against experimental temperature trajectories collected from the three temperature sensors (T1, T2, T3).  The calibrated parameters are: Model A – C_air = 616.56 J K⁻¹, G_box = 0.65 J K⁻¹; Model B – C_air = 486.12 J K⁻¹, G_box = 0.856 J K⁻¹, C_heater = 33.65 J K⁻¹, G_heater = 0.87 J K⁻¹.  Model B is preferred for predicting the heat‑bed inertia and is used in the Kalman‑filter based anomaly detector, in what‑if co‑simulation studies, and for controller optimisation.  Data are streamed from the physical twin (PT) via the low‑level driver to a RabbitMQ message broker; the DT consumes these streams for real‑time monitoring, calibration, and simulation.  The DT’s data management relies on message‑queueing (RabbitMQ) and a time‑series database for persistence, while model relationships are maintained through calibration algorithms that update the model parameters with the latest sensor data.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Actuation is performed by a fan and a heat‑bed.  The low‑level driver interfaces directly with the PT hardware and publishes actuator states to RabbitMQ; it also receives commands from the controller.  The controller implements a bang‑bang temperature controller with four tunable parameters: lower limit (LL), upper limit (UL), heating duration (H) and waiting duration (C).  Commands are sent and received over RabbitMQ, providing a remote control interface.  Actuation ranges are defined by the fan voltage (12 V in the baseline experiment, 18 V in the uniform‑temperature experiment) and the heat‑bed heating duration (H) and cooldown period (C).  Safety constraints are enforced by a safe protocol embedded in the low‑level driver, ensuring the PT remains within safe operating limits.  Actuator addressing is implicit in the RabbitMQ message routing (topic or queue names for fan and heat‑bed).

Sensors are mounted on the styrofoam box and read temperature periodically by the low‑level driver; the driver uploads the readings to RabbitMQ. Sampling frequency is not explicitly specified but is implied to be periodic (e.g., every few seconds) to support real‑time control. extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Calibration experiments were performed using the optimized sensor setup (Fig. 11) to generate a data trajectory. Model B demonstrated a better fit for the heat‑bed inertia compared to Model A. The calibration process and validation results are documented, and the calibrated parameters are used to update the DT models. No explicit confidence intervals or residual‑based re‑calibration procedures are described in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Temperature data from three DS18S20 1‑Wire sensors is read by the low‑level driver running on the Raspberry Pi 4 B. The driver polls the sensors at a fixed interval (periodic reads; the exact period is not specified in the documents) and publishes the readings to the RabbitMQ message broker as AMQP messages. Each message contains the sensor ID, timestamp, and temperature value in degrees Celsius. A safe‑protocol layer in the driver validates the integrity of the data before publishing. The DT components (controller, visualization dashboard, Kalman filter) subscribe to the relevant queues and consume the messages in real time. No additional preprocessing is performed beyond the safe‑protocol validation.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: The controller, implemented on the Raspberry Pi, receives temperature measurements from the DT via RabbitMQ and executes a bang‑bang control strategy. When the measured temperature falls below the lower limit (LL) or rises above the upper limit (UL), the controller publishes a command message to RabbitMQ. The low‑level driver consumes these command messages, validates them against the safe‑protocol, and translates them into GPIO signals that drive MOSFETs (isolated by optocouplers) to switch the heatbed and fan on or off. The driver also publishes the current actuator state back to RabbitMQ so that the DT can monitor the physical state. Command execution confirmation is implicit in the state‑update messages; no explicit ACK mechanism is described.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The DT’s communication backbone is a single‑node RabbitMQ broker running on the same Raspberry Pi 4 B that hosts the controller and low‑level driver. Components communicate over the local network using the AMQP protocol. The broker is configured for lightweight, in‑memory queues suitable for the low‑latency requirements of the incubator control loop. No external network interfaces are used; all traffic stays within the local network segment. The documents do not specify bandwidth, latency, redundancy, or security mechanisms; the broker is assumed to be unsecured and non‑persistent, relying on the reliability of the Pi’s local storage.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: The entire DT stack is deployed on a single Raspberry Pi 4 Model B. The Pi runs the RabbitMQ broker, the low‑level driver, and the controller logic. Sensor polling, message routing, and simple control logic are performed locally, eliminating the need for external cloud services. The deployment is on‑premise, edge‑based, and self‑contained. Scalability is limited to the Pi’s resources; for larger deployments, additional Pi nodes or a clustered RabbitMQ setup would be required, but such extensions are not described in the documents.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The incubator Digital Twin follows a multi‑phase lifecycle that mirrors the classic CPS development cycle. 1) **Physical Twin (PT) construction** – the insulated styrofoam box, heatbed, fan, Raspberry Pi controller, DS18S20 temperature sensors, MOSFET driver board and optocouplers are assembled and wired (see Fig. 3‑5). 2) **Model development** – two lumped‑parameter thermal models are derived; Model A uses two free parameters, Model B uses four, capturing the heatbed’s thermal inertia. 3) **Calibration** – controlled experiments are run and the parameters of Model B are tuned using a calibration algorithm that minimizes the error between measured and simulated temperatures. 4) **Digital Twin deployment** – a RabbitMQ message broker (AMQP 0‑9‑1) runs on the Pi, the low‑level driver publishes sensor data and receives actuator commands, the controller implements a bang‑bang logic with H, C, LL, UL parameters, and the DT streams data to a time‑series database for real‑time dashboards. 5) **Runtime monitoring & adaptation** – a Kalman filter estimates the unmeasured heatbed temperature and flags anomalies; a self‑adaptation loop schedules experiments, re‑calibrates Model B, re‑configures the Kalman filter and controller, and runs what‑if co‑simulations to optimise controller settings. 6) **Evolution & governance** – all code and model artefacts are versioned in a public GitHub repository (https://github.com/INTO‑CPS‑Association/example‑incubator), enabling traceability, peer review and incremental updates.  Transitions between phases are managed by the calibration and adaptation algorithms, which replace model parameters and controller settings while preserving the PT‑DT coupling.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The engineering process combines model‑driven development with continuous integration of data and algorithms.  Simulation models are built in a scientific computing environment (not specified but implied to be MATLAB/Simulink or Python), calibrated against experimental data, and then wrapped as services that subscribe to RabbitMQ topics.  The low‑level driver abstracts the 1‑Wire DS18S20 bus and MOSFET‑based actuator control, publishing temperature readings and receiving fan/heatbed commands.  The controller implements a simple bang‑bang logic with configurable limits (LL, UL) and dwell times (H, C).  Quality assurance is achieved through on‑demand calibration, real‑time anomaly detection via a Kalman filter, and what‑if simulations that co‑simulate PT and DT data streams.  Testing is performed by comparing live sensor data against the DT’s predicted temperatures, visualised on a dashboard.  Version control is handled by Git, with the repository hosting source code, model definitions, calibration scripts, and Dockerised RabbitMQ deployment.  Evolution is managed by the self‑adaptation loop: when an anomaly is detected, the system schedules a new experiment, re‑calibrates Model B, updates the Kalman filter, runs optimisation simulations, and finally re‑deploys the updated controller.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The DT provides several decision‑support capabilities: 1) **Real‑time status visualization** – a dashboard consumes time‑series data from RabbitMQ and displays current temperature, heatbed state, and controller status. 2) **Anomaly detection & heatbed state estimation** – a Kalman filter fuses Model B predictions with sensor measurements, yielding an estimate of the heatbed temperature and its covariance; thresholds on the innovation sequence trigger anomaly alerts.  3) **What‑if simulation** – co‑simulation of PT and DT data allows the user to vary controller parameters (H, C, LL, UL) and observe predicted temperature trajectories, facilitating optimisation.  4) **Self‑adaptation loop** – upon detecting a plant behaviour change, the system automatically schedules an experiment, re‑calibrates, re‑configures the Kalman filter and controller, and re‑optimises, closing the loop.  Decision making is performed by human operators via the dashboard or by automated scripts that apply optimisation results; confidence is quantified by the Kalman filter’s covariance and by statistical confidence intervals from calibration experiments.

The controller implements a bang‑bang control strategy with defined lower and upper temperature limits, heating duration, and waiting duration to prevent overheating. No explicit cybersecurity protocols, access control, or authentication mechanisms are described in the documents.

## The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering

Hao Feng _[†]_, Cl´audio Gomes _[†]_, Casper Thule _[†]_, Kenneth Lausdahl _[†]_,
Michael Sandberg _[†]_, and Peter Gorm Larsen _[†]_


_†_ DIGIT, Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, Aarhus
University, Denmark


February 23, 2021


**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

**Low-level driver** – Abstracts the low level communication with the sensors and actu
ators.

**Controller** – Implements the control logic required to regulate the temperature inside
the incubator. It receives the measured data and sends commands through the
communication server.


**2.2.1** **The Communication Server**


For the case study, the RabbitMQ [4] server was deployed for synchronisation and communication. With tens of thousands of users, RabbitMQ is one of the most popular
open source message brokers. RabbitMQ is a mature, lightweight, and easy to deploy
on premises and in the cloud.

The controller on the Raspberry Pi is listening from the RabbitMQ server for commands of controlling the fan and the heatbed and at the same time sharing the control
states to the RabbitMQ server.
We choose RabbitMQ because it greatly facilitates the digital twinning process, as
the DT can be implemented by components that listen to the messages being passed
between the controller and the load level driver, as illustrated in fig. 7.


**2.2.2** **The Low-Level Driver**


The low-level driver is used to interact with the PT and to issue the states of the PT

to the RabbitMQ server. In order keep the device in a safe state, a safe protocol is
incorporated into the low-level driver.
The low level driver periodically reads the temperature data, and checks if there are
commands from the controller, through the communication server. The temperature
data is uploaded to the communication server.


**2.2.3** **The Controller**


The controller used in the case study is a comparable to a bang-bang controller, which
a small difference described below. The control strategy is executed after receiving the

temperature measurements.
The principle of bang-bang control is that when a reference temperature is given
to the controller, the controller issues a heating signal turning on the heatbed until the
measured temperature from sensors reaches the reference temperature. Because of the
delayed effect of the temperature, the controller needs to wait after each actuation, to
make sure the temperature does not rise too much. Figure 8 shows the state chart of the

This constitutes our future work. Our goal is to implement a DT framework that enables **Real-time visualization of the incubator status** – achieved by listening to the controller and low level driver messages, and relaying that data into a time series database, and plot the data into a dashboard. **On demand calibration** – achieved by automatically running the calibration process described in section 3.3. This is similar to the tracking simulator implemented in [15]. **Anomaly detection and Heatbed state estimation** – This is achieved by running a Kalman filter [16] that uses Model B developed in section 3 and correlates the measure data to the (non-measured) heatbed temperature. **What-if simulations** – This is implemented by running co-simulations that relay the historical or real-time data from the PT (e.g., similar to what is done in [17]). It can be used to, e.g., optimize the controller parameters introduced in fig. 8. **Self-adaptation loop** – This is the functionality that truly _closes the loop_ of the DT. We envision that, whenever a new object (imagine a bucket of cold water) is placed in the incubator, the DT will: 1. detect that the plant behavior has changed (given by the anomalies detected by the Kalman filter); 2. schedule an experiment to gather relevant data (e.g., let the plant cool down to a safe temperature, then ramp up heating for some time). 3. configure the controller for the new experiment; 4. gather the experiment data; 5. run the calibration of Model B for new experiment; 6. re-configure Kalman filter with new parameters; 7. run what-if simulations to optimize the controller behavior; and 8. finally re-configure the controller. ### **6 Summary** We described the implementation and modelling of the incubator case study. **2.1.3** **The Fan** We installed a fan (see fig. 3(c)) inside the box to circulate the air inside the box for making the temperature as uniformly distributed as possible. The fan airflow is about 0 _._ 02m [3] s _[−]_ [1] . With a box volume of 0 _._ 03m [3], the fan should take 2 seconds to move all the air within the box. **2.1.4** **The Raspberry Pi** A Raspberry Pi 4 Model B (see fig. 3(d)) was selected to implement the controller. The General Purpose Input/Output (GPIO) connectors on the Raspberry Pi can be used to connect many accessories such as temperature sensors, fan, and the heatbed. Furthermore it supports wireless internet out of the box, with built-in Wi-Fi and Bluetooth, 5 which simplifies deployment of the controller. **2.1.5** **The Temperature Sensor** It is necessary to get feedback from the temperature sensors for feedback control. To do this, a DS18S20 High-Precision 1-Wire Digital Thermometer (see fig. 3(e)) was used for measuring the temperature. This temperature sensor can measure temperatures from -55 °C to +125 °C with an accuracy of 0.5 °C from -10 to +85 °C, which is suitable for the physical incubator. And each DS18S20 has a unique 64-bit serial code, which allows multiple DS18S20s to function on the same 1-Wire bus. Such 1-wire bus support to connect multiple sensors with only one GPIO. In the incubator system, three sensors were deployed for detecting the temperature. **2.1.6** **The PCB Design** Controlling the heatbed requires approximately 4A current which is not supported by the Raspberry Pi. Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

DTs increase the value of their physical counterparts by potentially enabling advanced visualisations, reconfigurability (and therefore robustness with respect to changing environment), safety, predictability, and reduced maintenance.
The often vague definitions and claims about DTs makes the concept hard to distinguish from: self-adaptive systems [7, 8], autonomic computing [9], Industry 4.0 [10],
models@runtime [11,12], and supervisory controllers [13].
We introduce the Incubator case study as an attempt to clearly identify the techniques used in digital twinning. This system is a simple, yet representative, CPS and
PT. Detailing how the DT is built is out of the scope of this manuscript, but we sketch
how such implementation can be done, and which technologies can be used. This
makes it easier for the reader to see the similarities of DT to the aforementioned re
lated research areas.

The main goal of the incubator is to reach a certain temperature within a box and
regulate it regardless of the content inside. The physical components in the incubator
form a plant that is controlled by a Raspberry Pi. The overall systematic diagram of
the Incubator is shown in fig. 2.

We start with a brief description of the hardware components, back-of-the-envelope
calculations, and refer the reader to the online repository [3] for more details.
The plant of the Incubator consists of (see fig. 3):

  - A styrofoam box in order to have an insulated container.

  - A heatbed to heat up the content inside the styrofoam box.

  - A fan to distribute the heating inside the box.

  - Two temperature sensors to monitor the temperature inside the box.

  - One temperature sensor to monitor the temperature outside the box.

**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative

develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).

Since systems are getting increasingly complex, models are needed to better comprehend their behaviour. A model is an abstract representation of a system, built with


1


the goal of understanding one aspect of the system [2]. For example, a Computer-Aided
Design (CAD) model of a car may store information about the sizes of the wheels,
shapes of the chassis, styles of the car, and so on, but it does not contain information
about how the forces acting on the car affect its movement. An engineer who desires
to analyse the dynamics of the car may build a dynamic model described by the differential equations introduced in, e.g., [3, Section 5].
Beyond better understanding, models can assist with optimising the overall system,
discovering causes and effects, measuring consequences of changes, and communicating among engineers. Once the system is deployed, the models used to build it should
not be discarded. Instead, they should be integrated into a Digital Twin (DT).
DT is often defined as a framework that integrates modelling, simulation, monitoring, and optimisation technologies, with the intent of adding value to the use of the
Physical Twin (PT) [4,5]. For instance, a DT provides the ability to run simulations that
reproduce and predict the behaviour of the PT under its current operating conditions.
The following important parts are highlighted [6]:
**Data** – collected from the PT through sensors over time;
**Models** – knowledge about different aspects of both the cyber and the physics of the
PT and its environment [1] ; and
**Algorithms** – techniques that use data and models, manipulating those to generate
more data and knowledge (e.g., fault detectors, supervisory controllers, state estimators, optimisers).
An example common structure of a DT is represented in fig. 1. In fig. 1, the DT
employs calibration algorithms to update the models of the CPS and environment with
the best estimate of the parameters. The resulting data is then used to inform monitors
that check whether the system is performing safely and optimally. When violations are
detected, humans (or other algorithms) may decide to make changes to the physical
twin.

## The Incubator Case Study for Digital Twin Engineering

Hao Feng _[†]_, Cl´audio Gomes _[†]_, Casper Thule _[†]_, Kenneth Lausdahl _[†]_,
Michael Sandberg _[†]_, and Peter Gorm Larsen _[†]_


_†_ DIGIT, Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, Aarhus
University, Denmark


February 23, 2021


**Abstract**


To demystify the Digital Twin concept, we built a simple yet representative
thermal incubator system. The incubator is an insulated box fitted with a heatbed,
and complete with a software system for communication, a controller, and simulation models. We developed two simulation models to predict the temperature
inside the incubator, one with two free parameters and one with four free parameters. Our experiments showed that the latter model was better at predicting the
thermal inertia of the heatbed itself, which makes it more appropriate for further
development of the digital twin. The hardware and software used in this case study
are available open source, providing an accessible platform for those who want to
develop and verify their own techniques for digital twins.

### **1 Introduction**


Cyber-Physical Systems (CPSs) are integrations of computation with physical processes. Embedded computers act through a network, monitor and control the physical
processes [1].
The cyber part refers to the computation part and communications, while the physical part refers to the part of the system interacting with the physical world. The environment is the part that may significantly affect the properties of the CPS but cannot
directly be controlled.
An example of a CPS is a car. The physical part in the car contains a body, four
wheels, seats, an engine, and so on, which interact with the environment (e.g., wind,
road surface). The cyber part of the car is different control systems implemented in
Electronic Control Units (ECUs) that communicate among themselves through a Controller Area Network.

The low-level driver is used to interact with the PT and to issue the states of the PT

The low level driver periodically reads the temperature data, and checks if there are

The controller used in the case study is a comparable to a bang-bang controller, which
a small difference described below. The control strategy is executed after receiving the

temperature measurements.
The principle of bang-bang control is that when a reference temperature is given
to the controller, the controller issues a heating signal turning on the heatbed until the
measured temperature from sensors reaches the reference temperature. Because of the
delayed effect of the temperature, the controller needs to wait after each actuation, to
make sure the temperature does not rise too much. Figure 8 shows the state chart of the
